beginner.question: How do you set the width of an element in CSS?
beginner.answer: Use the width property: width: 200px; (fixed pixels), width: 50%; (percentage of parent's width), width: 100vw; (viewport width), or width: auto; (default — browser calculates based on content and available space). Block elements default to width: auto, which fills their container's available width.

beginner.question: How do you set the height of an element in CSS?
beginner.answer: Use the height property: height: 300px; (fixed), height: 50%; (percentage of parent's height — but only works if parent has explicit height), height: 100vh; (viewport height), or height: auto; (default — browser calculates based on content). Block elements default to height: auto, which shrinks to fit the content.

beginner.question: What does width: 100% do and how is it different from width: auto?
beginner.answer: width: 100% sets the content width to exactly 100% of the parent's content width. width: auto (default for block elements) fills the available space but does NOT include margins, padding, or borders in that calculation. With auto, the total width = parent width — margins — padding — borders. With 100%, the content width = parent width, so adding margins/padding/borders will cause overflow.

beginner.question: What are the viewport units in CSS?
beginner.answer: Viewport units are relative to the browser's viewport size: vw (1% of viewport width), vh (1% of viewport height), vmin (1% of the smaller viewport dimension), and vmax (1% of the larger viewport dimension). For example, width: 50vw is half the viewport width, and height: 100vh is the full viewport height. These are useful for full-screen layouts and responsive designs.

beginner.question: What does min-height: 100vh do?
beginner.answer: min-height: 100vh ensures an element is at least as tall as the viewport, but can grow taller if its content exceeds that height. This is commonly used on main content containers to ensure the content area fills the screen even when there's little content, pushing footers down. Unlike height: 100vh, it doesn't cause content overflow — the element expands as needed.

beginner.question: What is the difference between min-width and max-width?
beginner.answer: min-width sets the smallest width an element can have — it won't shrink below this value, even if the container gets smaller. max-width sets the largest width — the element won't grow beyond this, even if the container is wider. They create a responsive range: .element { width: 100%; min-width: 300px; max-width: 1200px; } scales between 300 and 1200px.

beginner.question: What does height: auto mean?
beginner.answer: height: auto (the default) lets the browser calculate the element's height based on its content. The element expands vertically to accommodate all its content without clipping or overflow. For block elements with no content, auto results in zero height (unless padding/min-height is set). This is the most common setting for block elements as it allows natural content flow.

beginner.question: Why doesn't height: 100% work on child elements in many cases?
beginner.answer: height: 100% only works when the parent element has an explicit height set. If the parent's height is auto (the default), the percentage height cannot be calculated because the parent's height depends on its children's heights, creating a circular dependency. To use height: 100%, the parent must have a defined height like 500px, 100vh, or another percentage of a higher ancestor with defined height.

beginner.question: How do min-width, max-width, and width interact when all three are set?
beginner.answer: When all three are set, the browser determines the final width in this order: width sets the preferred width, but min-width is a lower bound (element can't be narrower) and max-width is an upper bound (element can't be wider). If width is less than min-width, min-width wins. If width exceeds max-width, max-width wins. So the actual width = clamp(min-width, width, max-width).

beginner.question: What is the default box-sizing value and how does it affect width calculations?
beginner.answer: The default box-sizing value is content-box. This means the width and height properties apply only to the content area — any padding, border, or margin is added outside the specified dimensions. For example, width: 200px; padding: 20px; border: 2px; results in 244px total width. Changing to box-sizing: border-box makes the width include padding and border.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between width: auto and width: 100% on a block-level element?
intermediate.answer: width: auto (default) calculates the element's width as the containing block width minus margins, padding, and borders. width: 100% sets the content area explicitly to 100% of the containing block width. With auto, margins, padding, and borders are subtracted to prevent overflow. With 100%, the content area is forced to 100%, so adding margins, padding, or borders causes overflow. Example: a div with width: 100%, margin: 20px will overflow its parent by 40px. With width: auto, it fits perfectly.

intermediate.question: How does the 'ch' unit work with max-width for readability?
intermediate.answer: The 'ch' unit equals the width of the '0' character in the current font. max-width: 65ch limits lines to approximately 65 characters wide, which is considered optimal for text readability (45-75 characters per line). This is a responsive approach — as font-size changes, the character count remains consistent. Example: article { max-width: 65ch; margin: 0 auto; } centers a readable article that adapts to font size.

intermediate.question: What problems can 100vw cause and how do you fix them?
intermediate.answer: width: 100vw can cause horizontal overflow because vw includes the vertical scrollbar's width. On Windows, where scrollbars take up ~17px, 100vw = viewport width + scrollbar width, causing a horizontal scrollbar. Solution: use width: 100% instead, which excludes the scrollbar and fits the actual available width. Alternatively, use overflow-x: hidden on the body (not recommended as it prevents scrolling).

intermediate.question: How does the min-height: 100vh sticky footer technique work?
intermediate.answer: Set the content wrapper to min-height: calc(100vh - footer_height) or use flexbox: body { display: flex; flex-direction: column; min-height: 100vh; } main { flex: 1; }. The main content area has flex: 1 which takes up remaining space, pushing the footer down. When content is short, min-height: 100vh ensures the body fills the viewport, keeping the footer at the bottom without overlapping content.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between setting height on html vs body?
intermediate.answer: Setting height: 100% on body alone doesn't work because the body's parent (<html>) doesn't have an explicit height. To use percentage heights on children, both html and body need height set: html { height: 100%; } body { min-height: 100%; }. Alternatively, use height: 100vh on body, which doesn't depend on the parent. The html element itself takes the viewport height when no height is set.

intermediate.question: How do you create a full-viewport-height hero section without causing overflow?
intermediate.answer: Use min-height: 100vh instead of height: 100vh to allow the hero to grow if its content exceeds the viewport height. If using height: 100vh and the content is taller, it will overflow and be clipped (overflow: hidden) or cause scrolling inside the element (overflow: auto). min-height: 100vh ensures at least full viewport height but expands naturally for larger content.

intermediate.question: How does the intrinsic sizing keywords min-content, max-content, and fit-content work?
intermediate.answer: min-content sizes an element to its minimum possible width — the width of the longest word or smallest intrinsic size. max-content sizes to the preferred natural width — the width needed to display content without any line breaks. fit-content acts like max-content but if the available space is smaller, it acts like min-content, and if larger, it uses the available space up to a maximum. These are useful for flexible sizing based on content.

intermediate.question: What happens when you set height on an element with overflow content?
intermediate.answer: When an element has a fixed height (e.g., height: 200px) and the content exceeds that height, the overflow behavior depends on the overflow property: overflow: visible (default) lets content overflow visibly; overflow: hidden clips the excess; overflow: auto adds scrollbars; and overflow: scroll always shows scrollbars. For text containers, it's often better to use min-height rather than fixed height to prevent clipping.

intermediate.question: How do you create a responsive square using only width/height properties?
intermediate.answer: Option 1: aspect-ratio property — .square { width: 50%; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; }. Option 2 (legacy): padding-bottom hack — .square { width: 50%; height: 0; padding-bottom: 50%; }. Both create a square that scales with the parent's width. The aspect-ratio approach is cleaner and allows content inside the square.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between setting width: min(100%, 800px) and max-width: 800px with width: 100%?
intermediate.answer: Both approaches can produce similar results, but they differ: width: min(100%, 800px) picks the smaller of the two values at the moment of computation, while max-width: 800px combined with width: 100% also ensures the element doesn't exceed 800px but is always 100% up to that point. The min() approach is more concise and can be more predictable when used in CSS functions like clamp() and calc().

advanced.question: Explain the complete algorithm for how the browser computes the used width of a block-level element in normal flow.
advanced.answer: The browser follows this algorithm: (1) Start with the containing block width. (2) Apply the formula: margin-left + border-left + padding-left + width + padding-right + border-right + margin-right = containing block width. (3) If width is auto and all other values have computed values, width becomes the remaining space. (4) If both margin-left and margin-right are auto with a fixed width, they split the remaining space evenly (centering). (5) If one margin is auto, it absorbs all remaining space. (6) If any values are over-constrained, margin-right is forced to auto.

advanced.question: How does the CSS height calculation differ for absolutely positioned elements with top and bottom set?
advanced.answer: For absolutely positioned elements with both top and bottom set, the height is determined by: top + margin-top + border-top + padding-top + height + padding-bottom + border-bottom + margin-bottom + bottom = containing block height. If height is auto, the element stretches to fill between top and bottom. If height is also set and there's a conflict, the bottom value is overridden. This allows absolutely positioned elements to be sized by their offset constraints rather than content.

advanced.question: What is the difference between the CSS intrinsic size algorithm and the extrinsic size algorithm?
advanced.answer: Intrinsic sizing determines an element's size based on its content (no external constraints) — used by min-content, max-content, and fit-content. Extrinsic sizing determines size based on external constraints (parent dimensions, viewport) — used by percentages, vw/vh, and fixed lengths. The browser chooses which algorithm to use based on the sizing properties: width: auto uses intrinsic sizing in some contexts (flex items, floats) and extrinsic in others (block elements in normal flow).

advanced.question: How do percentage heights work with CSS Grid and Flexbox containers?
advanced.answer: In Grid containers, percentage heights for grid items work differently: they are resolved against the grid area's size, which may be determined by the grid definition. In Flexbox, percentage heights on flex items resolve against the flex container's height if explicitly set. However, if the flex container's height is auto, the behavior depends on the align-items and stretch behavior — items may stretch to match the tallest item. Cross-axis percentage heights in flexbox can be circular and unpredictable.

advanced.question: Explain the 'definite' vs 'indefinite' size concept in CSS Grid and how it affects percentage-based child sizing.
advanced.answer: In CSS Grid, a size is 'definite' if it's explicitly set (e.g., grid-template-columns: 200px) or can be resolved without content (e.g., 1fr). A size is 'indefinite' if it depends on content (e.g., grid-template-columns: auto or no explicit row heights). When a grid track has indefinite size, percentage-based children cannot resolve their percentage against it — the percentage behaves like auto. This is a common cause of percentage heights not working in grid layouts.

advanced.question: How do the CSS containment properties (contain: size, contain: layout) affect width/height calculations?
advanced.answer: contain: size tells the browser that the element's size doesn't depend on its children, allowing the browser to skip layout calculations for descendants. The element must have explicit width/height (or the browser treats it as 0x0). contain: layout creates a new formatting context, preventing child margins from collapsing and containing floats. contain: paint clips children to the element's padding edge. Using containment can dramatically improve performance by limiting the scope of layout/paint recalculation.

advanced.question: Explain the 'replaced element' sizing rules for elements like img, video, canvas, and how they differ from non-replaced elements.
advanced.answer: Replaced elements have intrinsic dimensions (natural width/height) from their content. When only width is set, height auto-scales proportionally. When both are set, the content stretches (or uses object-fit). Unlike non-replaced elements, img { width: 50% } sets the image to 50% of the containing block, and height auto adjusts to maintain aspect ratio. Canvas has explicit dimensions via its width/height attributes. If only one dimension is specified on a replaced element, the other is computed to maintain the intrinsic ratio.
